content_en:
Umdah al-Ahkam min Kalam Khayr al-Anam (The Pillar of Rulings from the Words of the Best of Creation) by Imam Abd al-Ghani al-Maqdisi (d. 600 AH / 1203 CE) is one of the most beloved and widely studied hadith texts in Islamic education. The book collects 428 hadiths drawn exclusively from Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim — the two most rigorously authenticated hadith collections — and organizes them by legal topic. Every hadith in the Umdah thus carries the highest degree of authenticity, making it an ideal text for students learning both the hadith of legal rulings and the science of the chain of transmission.

Imam Abd al-Ghani al-Maqdisi was a major Hanbali scholar of the sixth century AH, born in the village of Jammain near Nablus in Palestine and educated in Damascus alongside his cousin Imam Muwaffaq al-Din ibn Qudama, author of al-Mughni. His selection of Bukhari and Muslim exclusively reflects the foundational principle of this text: to give students a memorizable, highly authentic core corpus of hadith from which fiqh rulings may be derived.

The book begins with the Book of Tahara (Purity) — the traditional opening of fiqh works — presenting the hadiths from Bukhari and Muslim that establish the rulings of purity in Islamic law. The opening hadiths include the famous statement of the Prophet, peace be upon him, that 'purification is half of faith' (Muslim), establishing the spiritual significance of purity before moving to its practical rulings. The hadiths on the types of water suitable for purification, the conditions of wudu, the description of the Prophet's wudu, and the hadiths on ghusl (full-body washing) are presented in sequence.

Students of the Umdah benefit not only from the hadith content but from observing how hadith are deployed to derive legal rulings. The text has been the subject of numerous commentaries, the most famous being that of Ibn Daqiq al-Eid (d. 702 AH), whose Ihkam al-Ahkam Sharh Umdat al-Ahkam remains a standard reference, and the commentary by Ibn Abd al-Hadi. Contemporary scholars such as Sheikh Fawzan and others have also produced accessible commentaries that make the text approachable for modern students.
